sections.text: Have you ever noticed that websites start with 'http://' or 'https://'? These aren't just random letters — they're the rules your computer and the server use to talk to each other. Think of it like a language: if you and your friend both speak English, you can understand each other. HTTP is the language that clients and servers use to communicate on the web!

sections.title: What is HTTP? 📨
sections.text: HTTP stands for HyperText Transfer Protocol. Don't let those big words scare you! It's just a set of rules that says 'here's how you ask for a web page' and 'here's how the server sends it back.' Without these rules, your computer and the server wouldn't understand each other — like trying to talk to someone who speaks a completely different language.

sections.title: What are HTTP Methods? 🎯
sections.text: HTTP has different types of requests called methods. The most common ones are GET (please give me something), POST (here's some new information), PUT (replace this existing thing), and DELETE (remove this thing). As a web developer you'll use all of these when talking to servers!

sections.title: The 4 Most Important HTTP Methods
items: GET — 'Give me this page/data.' Like asking a librarian for a book.
POST — 'Here's new data, please save it.' Like handing in a filled-out form.
PUT — 'Replace this existing thing with new data.' Like editing a saved document.
DELETE — 'Remove this thing.' Like asking the librarian to throw away a book.

sections.title: So What is HTTPS? 🔐
sections.text: HTTPS is the same as HTTP but with a HUGE bonus: it's encrypted! The 'S' stands for Secure. When you visit an HTTPS website, all the information sent between you and the server is scrambled so nobody else can read it. This is super important for things like passwords, credit card numbers, and private messages.

sections.title: HTTP vs HTTPS — The Postcard vs Letter Analogy ✉️
sections.text: HTTP is like sending a postcard — anyone who handles it along the way can read your message. HTTPS is like sending a letter in a locked box — only you and the person you're sending it to have the key. Always use HTTPS for anything private!

sections.title: HTTP Status Codes 🚦
sections.text: When a server responds, it sends a number called a status code to tell you how things went. 200 means 'Everything worked great!' 404 means 'I couldn't find what you asked for.' 500 means 'Something went wrong on my end!' You've probably seen a 404 page before!
🔒 Fun Fact: In 2010, most websites used HTTP. By 2023, over 95% of web traffic is HTTPS! Google even marks HTTP sites as 'Not Secure' in Chrome to warn you.

terms.definition: The set of rules clients and servers use to communicate on the web.
HTTP but encrypted and secure — scrambles your data so nobody can snoop.
An HTTP method for asking the server to send you some data.
An HTTP method for sending new data to the server.
A number the server sends back to say how things went (200=OK, 404=Not Found).
Scrambled so only the right person can read it.

questions.text: What does the 'S' in HTTPS stand for?
questions.explanation: The 'S' stands for Secure! HTTPS encrypts (scrambles) the data between your browser and the server so nobody else can read it.

questions.text: Which HTTP method would you use to ASK a server to GIVE you a web page?
questions.explanation: GET is used to request (get) data from a server. When you type a URL and press Enter, your browser sends a GET request!

questions.text: You see a status code of 404 on a website. What does it mean?
options: The page could not be found
questions.explanation: 404 means 'Not Found' — the server looked for what you asked for but couldn't find it. It's one of the most famous error codes on the web!

options: The rules clients and servers use to communicate
questions.explanation: HTTP (HyperText Transfer Protocol) is a set of rules — a communication protocol — that lets clients and servers understand each other when sharing web pages.

questions.text: Which is SAFER to use when entering your password on a website?
questions.explanation: HTTPS is much safer! It encrypts your data — like putting it in a locked box — so even if someone intercepts it, they can't read your password.
